1 - Lagging: You fail to establish version control or organizational standards in Figma; design deliverables frequently miss deadlines.
2 - Developing: You have begun implementing Figma naming conventions and component practices, but inconsistent adoption leads to occasional delays.
3 - Proficient: You have established clear Figma organization standards and component libraries that enable consistent, on-time delivery.
4 - Exemplary: You have architected a scalable design system with auto-layout and components that accelerates team velocity and supports rapid iteration.

1 - Lagging: You lack a systematic approach to capacity planning; design cycle estimation is unreliable and workload fluctuates unpredictably.
2 - Developing: You attempt capacity planning but struggle to balance UI/UX and multimedia workloads; estimates are occasionally accurate.
3 - Proficient: You have implemented effective capacity planning that achieves 80%+ accuracy in timeline estimates and keeps team workload balanced.
4 - Exemplary: You proactively forecast design capacity and prevent bottlenecks, enabling the team to support strategic initiatives while maintaining predictability.

1 - Lagging: You have not established design system standards; inconsistencies appear across screens and your teams lack color and typography guidelines.
2 - Developing: You have begun instituting design system standards, but inconsistencies still occur and accessibility compliance is incomplete.
3 - Proficient: You have established and enforced a comprehensive design system with documented standards for color, typography, and spacing across all outputs.
4 - Exemplary: You have built a fully tokenized, audit-compliant design system that demonstrates design excellence and enables consistent experiences across platforms.

Rating: Team Allocation (UI/UX vs Multimedia vs Research)
1 - Lagging: You have not aligned staffing with project needs; your 9 UI/UX designers and 3 research/multimedia staff are poorly allocated.
2 - Developing: You allocate resources reactively; occasional conflicts arise between UI/UX, multimedia, and research priorities.
3 - Proficient: You strategically allocate your 12-person team across UI/UX, multimedia, and research to support key initiatives.
4 - Exemplary: You have optimized resource allocation across your 9 UI/UX and 3 research/multimedia staff; the team works synergistically with dynamic staffing.

1 - Lagging: You consistently overload your team; design debt accumulates and capacity constraints force quality compromises.
2 - Developing: You attempt capacity planning but design sprints are sometimes overcommitted; workload management is reactive.
3 - Proficient: You proactively manage capacity, ensuring your team operates at sustainable utilization with buffer for emerging priorities.
4 - Exemplary: You employ data-driven capacity forecasting to balance workload, enabling both quality maintenance and design innovation within your team.

1 - Lagging: You have not invested in tool adoption; Figma, Adobe Suite, and ClickUp are underutilized or chaotically managed.
2 - Developing: You encourage tool use inconsistently; your team has partial adoption of design system in Figma and limited ClickUp integration.
3 - Proficient: You have established shared component libraries in Figma, integrated Adobe Suite into team workflows, and tracked design milestones in ClickUp.
4 - Exemplary: You have architected best-in-class design infrastructure with multi-file Figma libraries, Adobe automation workflows, and full ClickUp integration.

1 - Lagging: You have not communicated design progress; stakeholders lack visibility into timelines, constraints, and rationale.
2 - Developing: You occasionally update stakeholders on design progress, but communication inconsistency causes confusion about decisions and process.
3 - Proficient: You consistently communicate design status, feasibility, and rationale via ClickUp and Google Workspace; stakeholders understand constraints.
4 - Exemplary: You proactively engage stakeholders with documented design decisions and rationale; design thinking is communicated across the organization.

1 - Lagging: You have not aligned design expectations with reality; scope creep is common and expectations frequently diverge from feasibility.
2 - Developing: You sometimes clarify design scope, but expectations occasionally misalign during handoff and scope creep occurs.
3 - Proficient: You clarify design briefs upfront and define scope before kickoff; you manage scope changes through formal design review processes.
4 - Exemplary: You excel at expectation management through stakeholder interviews; you negotiate scope collaboratively with clear discussion of trade-offs.

1 - Lagging: You have not aligned design direction with product strategy; visual language is inconsistent with brand.
2 - Developing: Your design work sometimes diverges from product roadmap; brand consistency is improving but incomplete.
3 - Proficient: You ensure designs consistently reflect product vision; your design system tokens and guidelines align with brand standards.
4 - Exemplary: You have integrated design excellence into product strategy; your design innovations anticipate strategic direction and evolve brand thoughtfully.

1 - Lagging: You trace design issues to surface symptoms but fail to identify root causes; patterns repeat.
2 - Developing: You investigate design issues inconsistently; some root causes are identified, but prevention is not prioritized.
3 - Proficient: You analyze design issues systematically and document root causes to prevent recurrence in your team's work.
4 - Exemplary: You conduct comprehensive root cause analysis that prevents similar incidents; process improvements prevent future design problems.

1 - Lagging: You allow design problems to escalate uncontrolled with unpredictable response times.
2 - Developing: You eventually escalate design issues, but response times are inconsistent and stakeholders lack updates.
3 - Proficient: You escalate design issues appropriately and follow clear SLA; you keep stakeholders informed throughout resolution.
4 - Exemplary: You monitor proactively to prevent escalation and respond rapidly to design problems; clear communication is maintained throughout.

1 - Lagging: You do not enforce design QA; handoffs to development lack specs, assets, and verification.
2 - Developing: You attempt design QA but handoff documentation is sometimes incomplete; specs and values are occasionally missing.
3 - Proficient: You have instituted systematic design QA; handoffs are complete with specs, asset exports, and developer-ready prototypes.
4 - Exemplary: You conduct rigorous QA; handoffs are verified by dev; you track and iteratively improve design-to-code variance.

1 - Lagging: You delay design reviews; slow feedback turnaround blocks development and prevents design iterations.
2 - Developing: You conduct design reviews inconsistently; feedback delays occasionally impact project timelines.
3 - Proficient: You schedule regular design reviews and provide feedback within 48 hours; your team iterates efficiently without blocking development.
4 - Exemplary: You have implemented rapid review cycles with same-day feedback and asynchronous Figma comment processes that enable parallel work.

1 - Lagging: You provide vague design briefs; your team is unclear on objectives and multiple reiterations become necessary.
2 - Developing: You sometimes clarify briefs, but occasional rework is needed to define clear requirements.
3 - Proficient: You create comprehensive design briefs with defined acceptance criteria; your team proceeds with confidence.
4 - Exemplary: You craft exemplary briefs informed by user research; design objectives and success metrics are crystal clear upfront.

1 - Lagging: Your handoffs cause dev delays; missing specs, assets, or interactive elements require clarification.
2 - Developing: Your handoffs are occasionally imperfect; minor clarifications are needed from development.
3 - Proficient: You provide smooth handoffs with Figma links, complete specs, and documented design tokens ready for implementation.
4 - Exemplary: You have achieved frictionless handoffs where dev can build without back-and-forth through automated spec documentation.

1 - Lagging: You have not established a design system; components lack variants and styling is inconsistent.
2 - Developing: You have begun building a design system but it is incomplete; components lack documentation and manual overrides are common.
3 - Proficient: You have established a comprehensive design system with Figma components, variants for all states, and accessible documentation.
4 - Exemplary: You have built an exceptional design system that is fully tokenized with design-to-code handoff; you regularly audit and evolve it strategically.

1 - Lagging: You have not organized team files; Figma workspace is disorganized, Adobe files are scattered, and design decisions are undocumented.
2 - Developing: You have begun organizing files; Figma has basic structure and some design rationale is documented.
3 - Proficient: You have organized the Figma workspace well with naming conventions, structured Adobe file organization, and documented design decisions.
4 - Exemplary: You have created an exemplary system; Figma taxonomy is intuitive, Adobe workflows are optimized, and design decisions are comprehensively logged.

1 - Lagging: You conduct no design reviews; junior designers lack feedback and mentorship is absent.
2 - Developing: You conduct design reviews inconsistently; mentorship is sporadic and junior growth is uneven.
3 - Proficient: You conduct regular design critiques; senior designers mentor juniors with constructive, actionable feedback.
4 - Exemplary: You have built an exceptional mentorship culture with daily design pairing; junior designers rapidly develop skills and knowledge flows bidirectionally.

1 - Lagging: You have not tracked design debt; inconsistencies are hidden and your team lacks awareness.
2 - Developing: You track design debt informally; visibility is limited to core team members.
3 - Proficient: You log design debt in ClickUp and conduct regular audits to identify inconsistencies and refactoring needs.
4 - Exemplary: You have complete design debt visibility; automated audits flag component drift and dashboards display debt metrics.

1 - Lagging: You have not allocated resources to design debt reduction; your team operates in crisis mode.
2 - Developing: You occasionally dedicate sprints to design debt; allocation is inconsistent and time-limited.
3 - Proficient: You prioritize design debt reduction alongside feature work; backlog items are tracked and estimated.
4 - Exemplary: You strategically invest in design system health through regular refactoring sprints; debt reduction is balanced with new features.

1 - Lagging: You have not enforced design system adoption; new work frequently bypasses your system and introduces inconsistencies.
2 - Developing: You encourage design system adherence but some workarounds bypass components.
3 - Proficient: You have instituted design review gates in ClickUp that prevent non-compliant designs from progressing.
4 - Exemplary: You have achieved universal design system adoption; all new designs extend your system and demonstrate technical excellence.

1 - Lagging: You invest minimally in professional development; your juniors lack clear growth paths.
2 - Developing: You occasionally have growth conversations and offer some mentorship opportunities.
3 - Proficient: You provide structured growth plans; your designers receive regular feedback and you allocate learning budget.
4 - Exemplary: You excel at mentorship; you provide clear career paths and your designers rapidly skill-up in a psychologically safe environment.

1 - Lagging: You silo design knowledge; your team's insights are not captured or shared.
2 - Developing: You occasionally share design knowledge informally; structured sharing is absent.
3 - Proficient: You hold regular design lunches and document design patterns in Google Workspace; learning is accessible.
4 - Exemplary: You have built an exceptional knowledge culture; you archive design case studies, document research methodologies, and foster cross-team learning.

1 - Lagging: You do not invest in emerging tools or techniques; your team's skills stagnate.
2 - Developing: You provide some skill development; growth is slow and skill gaps remain.
3 - Proficient: You develop designer breadth through training in Adobe Suite, Figma components, and user research methods.
4 - Exemplary: You grow T-shaped designers with mastery in specialized areas and breadth across disciplines; you encourage R&D exploration.

1 - Lagging: Your team fears criticism; ideas go unvoiced and mistakes are hidden.
2 - Developing: Your team is somewhat comfortable sharing; some hesitation exists in design critiques.
3 - Proficient: You have created a safe environment where design exploration is encouraged; constructive feedback is accepted.
4 - Exemplary: You have built exceptional psychological safety; your team voices ideas freely, critiques are focused on work, and trust is evident.

1 - Lagging: Your team is disconnected from product impact; design work feels transactional.
2 - Developing: Your team sometimes sees design impact; connection to mission varies.
3 - Proficient: You tie design work to user outcomes; your team understands product vision and celebrates visible impact.
4 - Exemplary: You deeply invest your team in purpose; design work is celebrated as creating real user experiences; meaning is clear.

1 - Lagging: You experience high turnover; disengagement is evident and team morale is low.
2 - Developing: You retain talent adequately; some engagement fluctuations occur and occasional departures happen.
3 - Proficient: You maintain strong retention; your team is engaged with low unplanned turnover and enjoys the work environment.
4 - Exemplary: You have exceptional retention; zero unplanned turnover; your designers thrive and morale is consistently high.

1 - Lagging: You have not centralized design documentation; knowledge is scattered across Figma and Adobe files.
2 - Developing: You have some documentation; the repository is incomplete or difficult to access.
3 - Proficient: You maintain design knowledge in Google Workspace; patterns, research, and templates are accessible.
4 - Exemplary: You have built a comprehensive knowledge base with case studies, research databases, and design trends; searchable and well-maintained.

1 - Lagging: You limit design insights to your creative team; insights are not shared with product or engineering.
2 - Developing: You occasionally share design insights; cross-division collaboration is limited.
3 - Proficient: You share design research and insights with product and dev teams; design thinking influences roadmap decisions.
4 - Exemplary: You drive product decisions through design insights; you embed cross-functional design thinking across the organization.

1 - Lagging: You allocate no time or budget for design exploration; your team is purely reactive.
2 - Developing: You allow occasional design experiments; R&D time allocation is limited.
3 - Proficient: You allocate time for design exploration; your team documents experimentation and shares learnings.
4 - Exemplary: You foster a design innovation culture with regular R&D sprints; you celebrate experimentation and explore emerging trends.

1 - Lagging: Your design strategy is misaligned with business vision; your team is unclear on strategic direction.
2 - Developing: Your design direction is sometimes unclear; alignment with vision is incomplete.
3 - Proficient: You clearly articulate design vision; your visual strategy aligns with business goals.
4 - Exemplary: You align design strategy with business vision; your design direction influences strategic decisions.

1 - Lagging: Your design initiatives are reactive; you have no strategic design roadmap.
2 - Developing: You execute some strategic design projects; execution is inconsistent.
3 - Proficient: You maintain a design roadmap aligned with product strategy; you prioritize and execute strategic projects.
4 - Exemplary: Your design initiatives drive competitive advantage; your strategic projects lead the market and realize your vision.

1 - Lagging: You do not measure design impact; success metrics are absent.
2 - Developing: You track some design metrics; measurement is incomplete or inconsistent.
3 - Proficient: You track design metrics and measure impact on user satisfaction, conversion, and engagement.
4 - Exemplary: You comprehensively measure design impact through A/B testing; results inform iterations and impact is clearly quantified.

1 - Lagging: You leave design problems unresolved; decision-making is delayed or deferred.
2 - Developing: You eventually resolve design issues; decision-making is sometimes slow.
3 - Proficient: You address design issues promptly; you document decisions with clear rationale.
4 - Exemplary: You resolve design issues rapidly; you communicate decisions proactively and capture learning for future reference.

1 - Lagging: You identify design risks only when problems occur; proactive mitigation is absent.
2 - Developing: You sometimes identify design risks; mitigation is reactive.
3 - Proficient: You identify design risks early; you document and execute preventive mitigation strategies.
4 - Exemplary: You demonstrate exceptional risk foresight; you anticipate design risks and embed risk culture in your team.

1 - Lagging: You do not discuss design trade-offs; decisions appear arbitrary.
2 - Developing: You sometimes articulate trade-offs; reasoning is unclear.
3 - Proficient: You explicitly discuss design trade-offs with stakeholders; you present options with clear pros/cons.
4 - Exemplary: You transparently weigh design options; decisions are clearly explained and consensus is achieved.

1 - Lagging: Your design communication is unclear; misunderstandings are frequent.
2 - Developing: Your communication is sometimes unclear; occasional misinterpretations occur.
3 - Proficient: You communicate design decisions clearly; rationale is understood by stakeholders.
4 - Exemplary: You communicate exceptional clarity; design thinking is understood at all levels and stakeholders deeply grasp your direction.

1 - Lagging: Design feedback and questions go unanswered; responsiveness is poor.
2 - Developing: Your response is delayed; timeliness is inconsistent.
3 - Proficient: You answer design questions within 24 hours; responsive communication is standard.
4 - Exemplary: You have built a rapid response culture; stakeholder questions are answered same-day and communication is proactive.

1 - Lagging: You collect feedback ad-hoc; insights are not acted upon.
2 - Developing: You collect feedback sometimes; action is inconsistent.
3 - Proficient: You collect feedback regularly; stakeholder insights are integrated into your design process.
4 - Exemplary: You maintain exemplary feedback loops; insights drive improvements and feedback culture is continuous.

1 - Lagging: You ignore your team's skill gaps; no closure plan exists.
2 - Developing: You provide some skill development; gaps remain.
3 - Proficient: You identify skill gaps and provide training; capabilities are improving.
4 - Exemplary: You systematically close skill gaps; team capabilities are continuously elevated.

1 - Lagging: Your design processes are stagnant; improvements are rare.
2 - Developing: You make occasional improvements; adoption is inconsistent.
3 - Proficient: You regularly improve processes; you incorporate team feedback and optimize workflows.
4 - Exemplary: You drive continuous process innovation; improvements are implemented rapidly and efficiency gains are sustained.

1 - Lagging: Manual work dominates your team's process; automation opportunities are missed.
2 - Developing: You have implemented some automation; tool adoption is partial.
3 - Proficient: You automate design workflows where feasible; you leverage Figma and ClickUp APIs to improve efficiency.
4 - Exemplary: You employ best-in-class automation; design tools are integrated and workflows are optimized.

1 - Lagging: Your team resists change; new tools or processes are adopted slowly.
2 - Developing: Your team eventually adopts change; initial resistance is common.
3 - Proficient: Your team embraces change; new processes are adopted smoothly and feedback is incorporated.
4 - Exemplary: You demonstrate exemplary change leadership; your team enthusiastically adopts new approaches and innovation culture is evident.

1 - Lagging: You do not cast a clear design vision; team direction is unclear.
2 - Developing: You occasionally communicate design vision; clarity is limited.
3 - Proficient: You clearly articulate design vision; your team understands direction and alignment is visible.
4 - Exemplary: You cast exemplary vision; your design future is compelling, your team is inspired, and vision evolves thoughtfully.

1 - Lagging: Your design processes are either too rigid or chaotic; balance is absent.
2 - Developing: Balance between stability and agility is inconsistent.
3 - Proficient: Your processes provide structure while allowing flexibility; experimentation is enabled.
4 - Exemplary: You have achieved exceptional balance; your design system is stable and processes are agile; innovation and consistency coexist.

1 - Lagging: Your team morale is low; energy is depleted and motivation is lacking.
2 - Developing: Team morale is adequate; energy sometimes flags and engagement varies.
3 - Proficient: Your team morale is strong; team is energized and positive culture is evident.
4 - Exemplary: You have created exceptional team energy; your designers are eager to work and morale is consistently high.

1 - Lagging: Your team is dependent on you; empowerment is limited and growth is constrained.
2 - Developing: Your team is somewhat empowered; mentorship is sporadic and growth is uneven.
3 - Proficient: You empower your team to make decisions; mentorship is regular and designers grow visibly.
4 - Exemplary: You excel at empowerment; your team drives design direction and mentorship is exceptional.

1 - Lagging: You defend your position; team trust is limited and vulnerability is absent.
2 - Developing: Trust exists with reservation; vulnerability is limited.
3 - Proficient: You demonstrate trust and vulnerability; your team reciprocates and psychological safety is evident.
4 - Exemplary: You build exceptional trust; you are vulnerable about challenges and your team feels safe.

1 - Lagging: Your solutions are conventional; innovation is absent and patterns are reused.
2 - Developing: Your solutions are sometimes creative; innovation is occasional.
3 - Proficient: You encourage innovative design approaches; your team generates fresh solutions and design thinking is evident.
4 - Exemplary: You drive exceptional design innovation; your team produces breakthrough solutions and is known for creative excellence.

1 - Lagging: Blockers paralyze your team; creative workarounds are absent and problems fester.
2 - Developing: You address blockers eventually; resolution time is long.
3 - Proficient: You identify and address blockers creatively; your team finds workarounds quickly and maintains momentum.
4 - Exemplary: You excel at blocker resolution; your team finds creative solutions immediately and remains resilient.

1 - Lagging: You discourage experimentation; risk-aversion is high in your team.
2 - Developing: You allow some experimentation; fear of failure limits exploration.
3 - Proficient: You encourage experimentation in your team; failures are accepted as learning.
4 - Exemplary: You have built an exceptional experimentation culture; your team celebrates failures as learning and design risks are encouraged.
